key_departures.impact: Eight-year Saints captain and five-time All-Pro. Led New Orleans in tackles for eight consecutive seasons. Posted a career-high 143 tackles in 2025 at age 36. Massive leadership void in addition to production loss.

Four-year starter for Saints at CB/slot. Recorded 83 tackles, 11 PDs, and 2 INTs in 2025. Left for elite-money contract in Tennessee — deal that Saints were never going to match. Kool-Aid McKinstry and Quincy Riley remain as the CB starters.

Started 10 games at center after Erik McCoy suffered a season-ending pectoral injury in Week 7. Performed well (11 pressures allowed in 442 pass-blocking snaps). Departure leaves the Saints thin at center depth behind McCoy, who has significant injury history.

Saints franchise sack leader (132 career sacks) became UFA when his contract voided. Carried a $18.76M dead cap hit. Posted 10.5 sacks in 2025 — his most since 2021. Still unsigned as of March 13. Departure (if confirmed) creates a significant need at EDGE.
Rotational TE and locker room leader. His departure, combined with Taysom Hill's contract voiding, strips depth from an already thin TE room behind Juwan Johnson.

Contract voided at start of new league year. Versatile hybrid player used heavily at TE. Saints HC Kellen Moore spoke positively but vaguely about his future. Retirement possible. Departure is a notable depth loss at TE and utility player.
Depth TE. Minimal production impact but further thins a tight end room that is losing Moreau and possibly Hill.

Averaged only 37.3 net yards per punt, among the worst in the league. The Saints proactively moved on and traded him before replacing him in FA. Not a meaningful need departure given his poor performance.

signings.analysis: Top guard available in free agency on a legitimate four-year, $61M deal with $45M guaranteed and a $20M signing bonus. Edwards is a 16-game starter over each of the last two seasons with the Bills, earning praise for an offense that allowed a league-low 14 sacks in 2024. He slots immediately into the starting left guard role, a spot that has been a revolving door for years. With $45M fully guaranteed, this is a real investment — IOL moves from critical need to depth consideration. Erik McCoy (C) and Cesar Ruiz (RG) round out a now-solidified interior starting trio. Depth behind them is still thin, which is why Radunz was also re-signed.

Louisiana native returns home on a four-year, $52M deal — making him the seventh-highest-paid RB. Etienne rushed for 1,107 yards and 7 TDs in 2025 with the Jaguars and added 36 catches for 292 yards and 6 TDs. He brings elite-level dual-threat ability to a Saints offense that identified the run game as one of its biggest problems. The $13M AAV is elite-tier for the position. Four years of commitment signals he is the lead back. This also clouds Alvin Kamara's future (Kamara on the final year of his deal at $10.5M cap hit). RB is now a significant strength, not a need.

Saints reunite with a seventh-round pick who developed into a starter. Elliss, 30, led the Falcons in tackles in 2025 (107 tackles, 3.5 sacks, 10 TFLs, 1 INT) and was a team captain. The 3yr/$33M deal with $23M guaranteed is a real starter-money investment — it sits squarely in the starter AAV tier for the position. He is expected to be an immediate starter alongside Pete Werner. However, Elliss is not Demario Davis — he lacks Davis's elite leadership and production ceiling, and he himself is 30 entering his age-31 season. The need is substantially reduced but not fully eliminated given Davis's irreplaceable leadership presence and Elliss's age trajectory. LB drops from critical to moderate need.

A former first-round pick (20th overall, 2019) on a two-year, $8.75M deal with only $4.5M guaranteed. Fant had 34 catches for 288 yards and 3 TDs with the Bengals in 2025. He will pair with Juwan Johnson (who is returning), giving the Saints 12-personnel capability and blocking utility. The AAV ($4.4M) sits at depth-tier for TEs. However, the Saints lost Moreau, Hill (voided), and Jack Stoll — so while Fant adds a body, the overall TE room remains a concern. This is a depth/rotational add, not a resolution. TE remains a need for the draft.

Re-signing a depth rotational DT who spent most of 2025 on injured reserve (played in only 4 games). Two-year, $6.2M deal with just $1.65M guaranteed reflects his injury risk and limited production track record. He is a development piece with a good frame (6-5, 327 lbs) suited for the Saints' odd-front defense. Godchaux and Nathan Shepherd remain the primary starters. This keeps IDL depth intact but does not solve any starter-level concern.

A genuine need for the Saints who have cycled through four punters since Thomas Morstead departed after 2020. Wright, 26, averaged 49 yards per punt in 2025 — a massive upgrade over Kai Kroeger (37.3 net yards, among the worst in the league). The 4yr/$14M deal with $8M guaranteed is top-5 money at punter and represents a real long-term commitment. Addresses the position decisively. Special teams stabilized.

Re-signed on a two-year deal with no reported financial terms. Radunz started 10 games at left guard in 2025 after taking over for an injured starter, but PFF rated him 72nd of 79 qualifying guards. With Edwards signed as the starter, Radunz slots in as experienced, versatile depth who can play guard or tackle. Provides insurance for a position group that still has injury depth concerns. Contributes to IOL stability without addressing the starter question (already solved by Edwards).

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: David Edwards signing (4yr/$61M, $45M gtd) is a definitive starter-level investment at left guard. With McCoy at center and Ruiz at right guard, the starting three is now locked in. Depth is shored up by Radunz re-sign (2yr). IOL goes from a critical need to a low-priority, late-round add for depth. Center depth behind McCoy remains thin (Fortner left), so a Day 3 center pick is still worthwhile.

Travis Etienne Jr. signing (4yr/$52M, $28M gtd) eliminates RB as a meaningful draft need. He is the clear lead back at an elite AAV for the position. Alvin Kamara remains on the roster in the final year of his deal, providing depth. The Saints may still draft a late-round developmental back but RB is no longer a priority in any round.

Kaden Elliss signing (3yr/$33M, $23M gtd) partially addresses the hole left by Demario Davis's departure to the Jets. Elliss is a legitimate starter and his contract reflects real investment. However, he is 30 years old and lacks Davis's elite production ceiling and irreplaceable leadership presence. Pete Werner provides a starting partner. LB drops from a high need to a moderate one — a mid-round draft pick to develop depth/succession is still warranted.

Alontae Taylor's departure to Tennessee (3yr/$60M, $42M gtd) creates a clear CB need. Taylor was a four-year starter with 52 PDs since 2022 — the Saints have no like-for-like replacement on the roster. Kool-Aid McKinstry and Quincy Riley are now the projected starters at outside CB, but both are young with limited proven track records. CB becomes the No. 1 draft priority — a top-50 pick investment here would be appropriate. The Saints have reportedly identified this as their biggest remaining need.

TE situation has deteriorated, not improved. The Saints lost Foster Moreau (Texans, 2yr/$6.3M), Jack Stoll (Browns), and Taysom Hill (contract voided, status uncertain). Noah Fant's 2yr/$8.75M signing at depth-AAV partially mitigates the loss but does not constitute a full solution alongside Juwan Johnson. The Saints need additional TE help — this is a Rounds 2-4 draft target. Need stays at tier 2 given the depth of departures.

Cameron Jordan remains unsigned as of March 13. His status is the biggest remaining wildcard on the roster. Jordan posted 10.5 sacks in 2025 at age 36 — still productive, but a UFA on the open market and reportedly open to moving on. If Jordan departs permanently, EDGE becomes a critical need. Even if he returns on a one-year deal, his age means the Saints need a long-term answer. Chase Young is on the roster (restructured), providing some depth. EDGE remains a tier 2 need pending Jordan's resolution.

No free agency activity at WR. The Saints made zero additions at wide receiver despite it being an identified need. Juwan Johnson returns at TE. Rashid Shaheed was previously traded. The receiving corps around Tyler Shough remains a concern. WR remains a tier 2 draft priority — this is likely a Round 1 or 2 target at pick No. 8.

Ridgeway re-signed (2yr/$6.2M) but brings injury risk and limited proven production. Godchaux and Shepherd remain starters. Jonah Williams (UFA) is also still potentially re-signable. IDL is a depth need that can be addressed through Day 3 picks or second-wave free agency — no change to tier.

key_additions.headline: Top guard on the market ends years of revolving-door instability at left guard with elite-level commitment
Louisiana native returns home as the Saints' new franchise running back after 1,399 scrimmage yards and 13 TDs in 2025
Homegrown product returns to replace Davis, bringing 107 tackles, 3.5 sacks, and team captain credentials from Atlanta
New Orleans native and top-5 punter contract ends years of special teams instability at the position
Former first-round pick adds blocking and receiving depth alongside Juwan Johnson after TE room was stripped by departures
Saints retain interior DL depth piece on a two-year extension as they bet on health after injury-shortened 2025
Versatile veteran re-signed to provide experienced depth behind starter David Edwards along the interior line

key_losses.headline: Eight-year Saints captain and five-time All-Pro departs, leaving an irreplaceable leadership void after a career-high 143 tackles at age 36
Four-year starter and top-10 CB earns elite contract in Tennessee, creating the Saints' most glaring remaining roster hole
Emergency starter who replaced injured Erik McCoy and held down the center spot for 10 games departs to NFC South rival
Saints' all-time sack leader hits open market after contract voids; 10.5 sacks in 2025 but his return remains unresolved
Locker room leader and TE depth anchor departs, furthering concerns about the position group behind Juwan Johnson
Versatile hybrid player's contract voided at start of new league year; retirement and/or departure is possible
Bottom-of-league punter (37.3 net yds) traded away as Saints proactively upgraded the position with Ryan Wright

notes: Cameron Jordan's status is the most significant unresolved situation — he is a UFA as of March 11 and still unsigned as of March 13. His return or departure dramatically changes the EDGE need tier.
Taysom Hill's contract voided. His retirement or free agency departure is possible. Saints HC was vague about his future.
Alvin Kamara's future is unclear — his contract was restructured, signing Travis Etienne signals he may take a reduced or no role in 2026. He has a $10.5M cap hit on the final year of his deal.
Dillon Radunz re-sign terms (total value, guaranteed) were not publicly disclosed as of the updated_through date.
OTC pegged post-FA cap space at approximately $16M as of March 12-13, though this is a moving target as restructures and voided dead money continue to be processed.

Dead money context: The Saints are absorbing approximately $48M in dead cap from voided contracts (Jordan $18.76M, Hill $13.72M, Davis $14.29M, Moreau $1.4M) per ESPN reporting, which significantly constrains further spending.
